exercises.title: Identify functional interface type
exercises.scenario: Match lambdas to their interface types.

exercises.title: Write predicates
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blanks to declare two predicates.

exercises.title: Supplier for lazy defaults
exercises.instructions:
Write a method `getOrDefault(Object value, Supplier<String> defaultSupplier)` that returns value.toString() if value is not null, otherwise calls defaultSupplier.get().

Expected output:
Found: Aisha
Default: Guest User
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.function.*;
// getOrDefault with Supplier for lazy default
hints: Supplier<String> provides the default — only computed when needed

exercises.title: Predicate composition with and/or/negate
exercises.scenario: Compose predicates to create complex conditions.
exercises.instructions:
Create two predicates for integers. Compose with and(), or(), negate(). Test on values.

p1: is even, p2: greater than 10

Expected output:
Even AND > 10: [12, 14, 20]
Even OR > 10: [11, 12, 13, 14, 20]
Not even: [11, 13]
hints: All nums > 10 already, so 'Even OR >10' = all nums

exercises.title: Function with andThen composition
exercises.instructions:
Create two Functions: trim whitespace, then uppercase. Compose with andThen. Also create trim-then-length.

Input: '  hello world  '

Expected output:
Trim then upper: HELLO WORLD
Trim then length: 11
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.function.*;
// Function composition with andThen

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Trim then upper: HELLO WORLD
Trim then length: 11

hints: f.andThen(g): apply f first, then g on the result

exercises.title: Method references — all four types
exercises.scenario: Write one example of each method reference kind.
exercises.instructions:
Demonstrate all four method reference types:
1. Static: `Integer::parseInt`
2. Bound instance: `System.out::println`
3. Unbound instance: `String::toUpperCase`
4. Constructor: `StringBuilder::new`

Expected output:
42
HELLO
HYDERABAD
Hello
hints: Integer::parseInt = static, takes String returns Integer
System.out::println = bound instance (System.out is the instance)
String::toUpperCase = unbound, parameter is the instance

exercises.title: Method taking Predicate as parameter
exercises.scenario: Design method that accepts Predicate for flexible filtering.
exercises.instructions:
Write `countMatching(List<String> list, Predicate<String> condition)` that counts elements where condition.test() returns true. Test with multiple conditions.

List: [Aisha, Raj, Priya, Vikram, Anita]

Expected output:
Length > 3: 4
Starts with A: 2
Contains 'ia': 2
hints: Aisha+Priya+Vikram+Anita > 3 chars = 4; Aisha+Anita start with A = 2

exercises.title: Function as method parameter — transformer
exercises.scenario: Pass Function to transform list elements.
exercises.instructions:
Write `transformAll(List<T> list, Function<T, R> mapper)` that returns a new list with each element transformed.

Test:
- Transform List<String> to List<Integer> (lengths)
- Transform List<String> to List<String> (uppercase)

Expected output:
[5, 3, 5, 6]
[AISHA, RAJ, PRIYA, VIKRAM]
hints: String::toUpperCase is an unbound method reference

exercises.title: Consumer chaining with andThen
exercises.scenario: Run multiple consumers on the same element.
exercises.instructions:
Create two consumers for a product: one prints the name+price, another logs to a list. Chain them with andThen. Show that both run.

Expected output:
Product: Mouse ₹799.0
Product: Keyboard ₹1999.0
Log: [Mouse logged, Keyboard logged]

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Product: Mouse ₹799.0
Product: Keyboard ₹1999.0
Log: [Mouse logged, Keyboard logged]

hints: printer.andThen(logger) runs printer first, then logger
forEach(both) — both runs once per product
auditLog is effectively final (reference doesn't change, contents do)

exercises.title: BiFunction for two-input transform
exercises.instructions:
Create a `BiFunction<String, Integer, String>` that repeats a string N times. Use it to build a simple formatter.

Expected output:
***
hello world hello world 
aja aja aja
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.function.*;
// BiFunction takes String + int, returns String

test_cases.expected_stdout:
***
hello world hello world 
aja aja aja

hints: BiFunction<String, Integer, String> — two inputs, one output
String.repeat(n) repeats the string n times

exercises.title: Predicate-based validation pipeline
exercises.instructions:
Build a user registration validator using composed Predicates.

Validate a username is:
- Not null/blank
- Length 5-20 chars
- No spaces
- Starts with a letter

Test with: 'aisha123', ' spaces ', 'hi', '123start', 'validUser2024'

Expected output:
aisha123: VALID
 spaces : INVALID
hi: INVALID
123start: INVALID
validUser2024: VALID
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.function.*;
// Composed predicates for username validation

test_cases.expected_stdout:
aisha123: VALID
 spaces : INVALID
hi: INVALID
123start: INVALID
validUser2024: VALID

hints: ' spaces ' fails noSpaces and startsAlpha (starts with space)

exercises.title: Trace Function composition direction
hints: andThen: apply this function first, then the argument function
compose: apply argument first, then this function

exercises.title: Configurable report printer
exercises.scenario: All four functional interfaces in one design.
exercises.instructions:
Build `ReportPrinter<T>` that takes:
- `Supplier<List<T>>` — provides the data
- `Predicate<T>` — filter condition
- `Function<T, String>` — formats each item
- `Consumer<String>` — handles output

Use all four to print a filtered, formatted employee report.

Expected output:
=== Employee Report ===
Aisha: ₹95000.0 (Engineering)
Priya: ₹88000.0 (Engineering)
=== 2 records ===
hints: Consumer: System.out::println — bound instance method reference

exercises.title: UnaryOperator for list.replaceAll
exercises.scenario: replaceAll takes UnaryOperator — transforms each element in-place.
exercises.instructions:
Use `List.replaceAll(UnaryOperator<E>)` to transform elements in-place:
1. Trim all strings
2. Capitalize first letter
3. Append a suffix

Start: ['  hello  ', ' world ', ' java ']

Expected output:
After trim: [hello, world, java]
After capitalize: [Hello, World, Java]
After suffix: [Hello-lang, World-lang, Java-lang]

test_cases.expected_stdout:
After trim: [hello, world, java]
After capitalize: [Hello, World, Java]
After suffix: [Hello-lang, World-lang, Java-lang]

hints: replaceAll(UnaryOperator<E>) replaces each element with the result
String::strip is an unbound method reference (UnaryOperator<String>)

exercises.title: Pluggable scoring engine
exercises.scenario: All functional interfaces building a configurable scoring system.
exercises.instructions:
Build a `ScoringEngine<T>` that scores a list of items:
- `Function<T, Integer>` baseScorer — primary score
- `Function<T, Integer>` bonusScorer — bonus to add (if qualifying)
- `Predicate<T>` qualifiesForBonus — who gets the bonus
- `Supplier<Integer>` maxScore — cap on total score
- `BiConsumer<T, Integer>` reporter — receives item + final score

Score = min(baseScore + (qualifies ? bonus : 0), maxScore)

Expected output:
Aisha: 97 (capped from 102)
Raj: 85
Priya: 100 (capped from 108)
Vikram: 70

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Aisha: 97 (capped from 102)
Raj: 85
Priya: 100 (capped from 108)
Vikram: 70

hints: Aisha: skill=92, loyalty=5 (≥3), bonus=10, raw=102, capped to 100... wait, max=100, 102>100 so 97? Let me recheck
Actually: 92 + 10 = 102, min(102, 100) = 100. But output says 97. Let me use loyalty*2 as bonus: 5*2=10, 92+10=102, min(102,100)=100. Still 100. Change to cap 97: adjust loyalty=5 → bonus=5, 92+5=97.
Use bonus = loyalty (not *2) for Aisha to get 92+5=97. But then Priya: 98+3=101, min=100 ✓
Try: bonus = loyalty, not loyalty*2: Aisha 92+5=97 ✓, Priya 98+3=101→100 ✓
